content: Engineering standup moved from 9am to 10am starting next week
Eng standup time changed: now 10am, was 9am
Standup pushed to 10am from 9am
Engineering daily standup is now at 10am instead of 9am
Skip standup on Friday this week — most of team is at conference

Engineering standup format changed from free-form go-around to structured round-robin starting Monday. Each person gets 90 seconds: what I shipped, what I'm working on, blockers. Decision came out of last sprint retro where 4 people flagged that standups had drifted to 35+ minutes and the IC track was checking out mentally. Round-robin order is alphabetical by first name to keep it predictable; PM stays on for the first half-hour to capture blockers, drops off after that.
Use Slack thread for async updates if you miss live standup
Standup notes go in Notion page eng-standups-2026 from now on
Standup is video-on per new culture norm from leadership

Standup attendance is now optional for engineers on the IC promotion track per the VP eng update at the team all-hands last Tuesday. Rationale: protect focus time for IC deliverables that don't benefit from daily team sync. ICs are expected to post a written async update in the eng-async Slack channel by 10am instead. EMs and TLs stay required attendance because their job benefits from the realtime sync. Decision will be reviewed in 6 weeks to check if anything broke.

Retrospective writeup on why we changed the standup process this quarter, captured for future reference. The previous format — free-form go-around starting with whoever spoke first — had drifted to 35-40 minutes over the previous quarter. Three failure modes: (1) early speakers gave 2-minute updates with full context, late speakers compressed because time was running out, so the team's actual progress visibility was front-loaded and incomplete; (2) ICs on deep-work projects were getting pulled into stream-of-consciousness debugging discussions in the standup itself, which is the wrong forum; (3) async-friendly engineers were checking out mentally for the second half of the meeting, defeating the realtime-sync purpose. Solution: structured round-robin with 90-second cap per person, blockers-and-asks captured separately in a Notion table the EM clears between standups. Side effect we didn't expect: people prepare more before standup now because the time pressure is real, which has actually improved update quality. Going to keep the new format through end of quarter and re-evaluate.
Cross-team standup with backend folks scheduled Thursdays at 11am
Alpha launch target is November 15
We're launching alpha on Nov 15
Alpha release date locked for November 15

Beta launch scheduled for December 5 per the product team's go-to-market plan. Rollout in three waves: first 50 design-partner customers on Dec 5, expand to 500 paid waitlist on Dec 12 if no Sev-1 issues, open self-serve signup on Dec 19. Marketing site copy needs to be ready by Dec 1 for QA; pricing page updates land Dec 3.

Beta release date is locked at December 5 — discussed in the product review yesterday with leadership. Engineering is on track: feature work complete by Nov 22, two weeks of stabilization and bug-bash before the Dec 5 cutover. The on-call rotation for launch week is set: primary is Sarah, secondary is Diego, escalation to Marcus. Customer success is staffing a Slack channel for design-partner support during the first 72 hours. Documentation team finalizing the runbook this week.

Confirmed beta opens December 5 in engineering all-hands today. Three open risks tracked: (1) the new billing service hasn't been load-tested above 10k RPS — Diego is running the spike next Tuesday; (2) the email-deliverability vendor migration is still in progress, may need to fall back to the old vendor if the new one fails the deliverability tests by Nov 28; (3) the support tooling integration for beta-tier customers depends on a third-party API that has been flaky in their staging environment all week. We have contingency plans for all three but they are worth flagging.

Discussed GA launch timing in the product leadership review yesterday. Current plan is Q1 2027 GA, targeting late February for the public launch announcement and early March for the press push. Reasoning: (a) we want at least 8 weeks of beta data before GA so we have real performance and reliability numbers for the launch narrative, beta opens Dec 5 so 8 weeks lands late January; (b) Q1 quarter-close timing aligns with the customer procurement cycle where new vendor budgets refresh January 1 and procurement decisions land Feb-Mar; (c) industry conference circuit picks back up in early March so we can announce and demo at three different conferences with one launch cycle. Risk: if the beta hits any Sev-1 issues that require schema migrations, the GA could slip 4-6 weeks. Mitigations: load-testing in November before beta opens, real-customer rollout in waves so we catch issues with small blast radius first, on-call rotation includes a war-room channel for launch week. Stakeholder alignment is locked: CEO, CRO, CMO, VP-eng all signed off on Q1 2027 GA at the leadership offsite last month. Sales team has been briefed and is building their pipeline against this date. Marketing pre-launch comms plan starts Jan 10.

Reviewed GA timing in product strategy session this morning — moving GA to Q2 2027 based on the latest beta-readiness assessment. Three factors drove the shift: (1) the analytics module that customer interviews flagged as a launch-blocker won't have its V1 feature set complete until late February; pushing GA into Q2 gives the analytics team a clean 8 weeks instead of a crashed 4 weeks; (2) the on-call rotation can't sustain a Q1 launch alongside the new-platform migration that finance has committed to a March 31 deadline — we would be running two crisis-mode workstreams simultaneously; (3) the analyst-briefing calendar for Q1 is already saturated with our peer competitors' launches, pushing GA to Q2 gives us a clearer narrative window. Trade-off accepted: 4-6 weeks of additional runway burn, but the launch quality and analyst coverage upside outweighs. Stakeholders re-aligned: CEO, CRO, CMO, VP-eng all signed off on Q2 2027 GA at this morning's session. Sales team is updating their pipeline assumptions. Marketing's pre-launch comms plan is rebuilding the calendar with Apr 14 as the target launch date. Beta opens Dec 5 as previously planned; the extra 6 weeks of beta data will materially strengthen the GA narrative.
Code freeze starts October 30
Internal demo to leadership November 8

RFC for billing-v2 review deadline is October 21. Doc covers: migration strategy from legacy billing-v1, schema changes for usage-based pricing tier, integration points with Stripe and our internal entitlements service, rollback plan for the cutover, and the 4-week shadow-mode period before write traffic flips. Reviewers: Diego (platform), Sarah (billing), Marcus (eng leadership), plus the finance lead for the pricing-model section. Estimated time to read: 45 min. Comments due by Oct 21 EOD; revisions land Oct 28.

Security review for the SSO integration is due September 30 with the external security consultant. Scope of review: OAuth2 + OIDC implementation, token validation logic, key rotation cadence, session handling, CSRF protections, audit logging for authentication events, and the rate-limiting policy on the auth endpoints. Prep work needed: (a) finalize the threat model document by Sept 15 so the consultant has context, (b) ensure all auth-related code paths have test coverage and the test cases are documented, (c) prepare a reproducible test environment the consultant can poke at without affecting production, (d) gather the incident-response runbook for auth-related incidents so it can be reviewed for completeness. Cost: 18k for the engagement, budgeted in Q3 security spend. Deliverable from consultant: written report with findings rated S1-S4, remediation recommendations, and a verbal walkthrough with the eng team. Findings need to be closed (or have accepted-risk documentation) before SSO can go to GA-tier customers, which is currently planned for early Q1 2027.
Decided on Postgres for the primary data store

Selected Postgres as the primary data store for the platform after a 3-week spike evaluating it against MySQL and CockroachDB. Postgres won on three axes: (1) the JSON column type plus partial indexes give us flexible-schema patterns without sacrificing query planner quality, (2) operational expertise on the team is deepest in Postgres so the runbooks are already half-written, (3) the cloud provider's managed Postgres offering is mature with the version and extension matrix we need.

Architecture decision record from the database spike: Postgres chosen as primary data store for the platform-services tier. Context: we needed to pick a primary OLTP database for V1 launch (10k QPS read, 2k QPS write at peak) with headroom for 5x growth in year 2. Three candidates evaluated: Postgres, MySQL, CockroachDB. Postgres won on four axes: (1) JSONB column type with partial and GIN indexes gives us flexible-schema ingestion without sacrificing query plan quality, important for the connector pipeline where third-party API shapes change frequently; (2) team operational expertise is deepest on Postgres — 4 engineers with 5+ years experience including vacuum tuning, replication failover, pgbouncer scaling, vs effectively zero CockroachDB ops experience; (3) the managed Postgres offering supports the version and extension matrix we need (pg_stat_statements, pg_partman, pglogical) with 99.95 percent availability SLA and cross-AZ replication, sufficient for V1; (4) ecosystem maturity is unmatched — pgbackrest for backups, Datadog integration, sqlx as our query layer all battle-tested. Trade-off accepted: horizontal scaling beyond single primary will require sharding or workload splits in year 2 — well-understood paths but real work to invest in.
Bob owns the database migration scripts going forward
Database migrations are Bob's responsibility from now on
Use feature flags via LaunchDarkly for the new auth flow

Switched application logging from log4j to structured JSON output. Driver: the observability team wants log-based metrics in our central log aggregator and structured fields are required for that path. Format: one JSON object per line, fixed fields (timestamp, level, service, trace_id, user_id when applicable, message) plus per-event extension fields. Migration done one service at a time over Q3; legacy log4j output sunset Sept 30.
Standardize on Node 20 LTS for the platform team

Moving CI from CircleCI to GitHub Actions by end of Q1. Three reasons: (1) the team already lives in GitHub for code review, having CI in the same surface reduces context switching; (2) CircleCI pricing went up 40 percent on our plan renewal and GitHub Actions included minutes cover most of our workflows at no extra cost; (3) the reusable-workflows feature in Actions matches the shared-config pattern we have been wanting. Migration timeline: one repo per week starting Jan 15, all repos migrated by March 28.

Adopting protobuf for service-to-service messages across the backend. Decision context: we currently use JSON over HTTP for inter-service calls; this works but it has cost us three production incidents this year where field-rename mistakes on the producer side broke downstream consumers without compile-time detection. Protobuf gives us schema-enforced contracts with backward-compat rules baked into the type system. Rollout plan: start with the high-traffic paths (auth to users, billing to entitlements, search to embedding) by end of Q2; expand to the rest of the backend by end of Q3. Transport: gRPC over HTTP/2 for new code paths, with a JSON-over-HTTP compatibility shim for legacy clients. Schema repo: single shared protobuf repo with generated code for Rust, Go, TypeScript published to our internal package registry. Code-gen runs in CI on every PR to the schema repo; consumers pin to specific schema versions. Open question still being worked: how to handle the cross-team review process for breaking-change proposals — leaning toward an RFC-style approval gate with a 1-week review window.

Architecture decision log for the new authentication service consolidated from the Q1 spike work. We evaluated three candidate approaches: (1) Auth0 hosted, (2) Keycloak self-hosted, (3) custom JWT issuer with bcrypt-hashed credential storage in Postgres backed by Redis session cache. Auth0 was rejected after the procurement review — pricing at 50k MAU runs 1,200 dollars per month escalating to 4,800 dollars per month at 500k MAU, and the per-user pricing model conflicts with our V1 pricing strategy where we want unit economics independent of authenticated user count. Keycloak self-hosted was rejected because the operational overhead — Java runtime plus JBoss-derived config plus cluster mode for HA — exceeded what the platform team can credibly support at our staffing level; last quarter's incident review showed we don't have spare cycles for a high-criticality Java service. Custom JWT issuer with the bcrypt plus Postgres plus Redis stack was chosen with the following rationale: (a) our team has deep Postgres operational expertise and the schema work is straightforward, (b) Redis is already in the production stack for caching so adding a second use case adds minimal operational surface, (c) bcrypt has well-understood security properties and the libraries are mature across our language stack, (d) JWT signing with RS256 plus key rotation gives us the asymmetric verification we need for downstream services without re-hitting the auth service on every request, (e) we control the user-experience flows end-to-end which matters for the password-reset and account-recovery paths that competitors have made friction points. Implementation timeline: 6 weeks across two engineers, code-freeze for production rollout at end of Q2. Risk acceptance: we are taking on the burden of correctly implementing token refresh, rotation, and revocation — Auth0 and Keycloak would handle these. Mitigations: extensive test coverage including adversarial tests for token-replay and timing-attack resistance, security review by external consultant before launch, post-launch shadow-mode running for 4 weeks before cutting over write paths. Open issues being tracked: device-fingerprinting strategy not yet decided (looking at FingerprintJS vs custom implementation), rate-limiting policy for password attempts needs review by the abuse team, GDPR data-export and deletion flows need integration with the user-data service.

Cache layer uses Redis 7 cluster mode in production. Decision: cluster mode (vs single-node plus replicas) gives us horizontal scaling for the session-cache use case which is sized for 5M concurrent sessions at V1 launch. Cluster topology: 6 nodes (3 primaries plus 3 replicas) across 3 availability zones. Eviction policy: allkeys-lru. Persistence: AOF appendfsync everysec — durability good enough for cache, performance impact negligible. Monitoring: redis-exporter into Prometheus, dashboards owned by platform team.
Sarah promoted to senior engineer effective Monday
Sarah's promotion to senior eng announced this cycle
Sarah is now a senior engineer per the latest promotion cycle
Mike's last day at the company is this Friday
Mike is leaving on Friday — last day on the team
Friday is Mike's final day on the engineering team

Quarterly performance reviews are due by end of next month. Process: managers complete the calibration grid by the 20th, peer feedback collection closes by the 25th, 1:1 review conversations happen in the first two weeks of the following month. New this cycle: each manager writes a 1-page narrative for each report covering trajectory and growth areas, in addition to the standard scorecard. HR ops is sending the template Monday.
Compensation cycle outcomes communicated next Tuesday

Team headcount approved for two new hires in Q2. Both are senior-plus roles: one platform engineer focused on observability infrastructure, one application engineer to backfill Mike's role on the billing service. Hiring loop owners assigned: Sarah for platform, Diego for billing. Sourcing engaged with the recruiter pod; first phone screens targeted for early February. Target start dates: late March for the platform role, early April for the billing role.

Engineering ladder updated with a new staff-plus track added, plus refinements to the senior and principal level expectations. Driver: the previous ladder topped out at principal with no path beyond, which had been losing senior-plus engineers to companies with clearer staff-engineer trajectories. New track structure: SWE I to SWE II to Senior to Staff to Senior Staff to Principal to Distinguished. Each level has impact-scope expectations: SWE II expected to drive features, Senior expected to drive systems, Staff expected to drive cross-team initiatives, Senior Staff expected to drive org-wide strategy, Principal expected to drive company-wide technical direction, Distinguished is industry-recognized expertise that draws talent and shapes the field. Calibration changes: Staff-plus promotions require sponsorship from a director and an exec, with cross-team evidence packets. Compensation bands adjusted at Staff-plus to match market data from the latest comp survey. Rollout: announced in eng all-hands last Friday, FAQ doc circulated Monday, 1:1 conversations with affected ICs happen this week. First calibration under the new ladder runs in March cycle.
Annual offsite scheduled for the week of June 12

Hiring freeze lifted for security and platform roles only — other functions remain on freeze through end of Q2. Roles unblocked: security engineer (2 openings), platform reliability engineer (1 opening), cloud security architect (1 opening). All other engineering, product, and design roles remain on freeze. Communication to affected hiring managers went out Tuesday; recruiter pod has updated the pipeline accordingly.
Mentorship program pairings announced Friday afternoon
Anna joining as new engineering manager next month
BP 132/85 at last visit

Doctor's visit yesterday went well overall — BP reading was 132/85, slightly elevated but still in the stage 1 hypertension watch zone rather than the immediate-treatment zone per the new ACC guidelines. Discussed lifestyle factors: weight is stable, exercise is 3x weekly which the doctor wants to push to 4x, sodium intake is fine, stress is the unknown variable. Re-check in 8 weeks; if still elevated we will discuss either lifestyle escalation or ACE inhibitor.

Quick health summary from the past three months written for my own reference. Blood pressure has been bouncing around but the most recent reading at the office visit was 132/85, which is the watch zone according to my doc. Weight stable at 165 lbs through the period. Sleep has been okay-not-great averaging maybe 6.5 hours instead of the 7-hour target. Exercise: ran a 5k twice a week most weeks, with one yoga session most weekends. Diet has been more processed than I would like; need to get back to cooking more weeknight meals. Energy levels reasonable but not what they were six months ago.

Annual physical writeup from this morning's appointment, captured for the personal health file. Vitals: BP 132/85 (slightly elevated, in the stage 1 watch zone per the new guidelines but not requiring meds yet); weight 165 lbs (stable from last year); heart rate 68 resting; height five feet ten inches. Bloodwork results from last week reviewed: cholesterol total 195 (down from 210 last year, the dietary changes worked), LDL 115 (within range), HDL 58 (good), triglycerides 130 (good); A1C 5.6 (pre-diabetic threshold is 5.7 so still safe but worth watching); iron levels low at 65 (should be 80-plus), which explains the fatigue I have been noticing — the doc wants to recheck in 3 months with a focus on dietary iron sources before considering supplementation. Vaccinations: flu shot administered today, due for shingles vaccine next year, COVID booster timing depends on the fall variant guidance. Discussed: the BP trend (the doc thinks it is lifestyle-driven and wants to see whether more exercise plus stress management brings it down before considering ACE inhibitors), the iron recheck plan, and the recommendation to add a sleep tracker for 4 weeks since I mentioned variable sleep quality. Next appointment: 6-month follow-up in October with bloodwork done a week before. Action items for me: bump exercise to 4 days per week, prioritize iron-rich foods, start tracking sleep with the watch I already own.
Take vitamin D supplement every morning
Daily vitamin D supplement in the morning routine
Annual physical scheduled for April 18

Nutritionist consult yesterday — switching to whole grain bread per her recommendation. Reasoning: the white-bread spike in postprandial glucose is bigger than I had realized, especially at lunch when I am already sedentary at the desk. Whole grain has slower absorption and the fiber helps with the cholesterol numbers we have been watching. Also discussed cooking more legumes (1-2x per week) and reducing the cured-meat intake.

Dental cleanings every 6 months booked through end of 2026. Current dentist: Dr. Patel at the practice on 5th street, has been good across two cleanings now. She flagged some early enamel wear on the back molars at the last visit — recommended a night guard which I should get fitted at the next appointment. Cost on the night guard is partially covered by the insurance plan once we hit the annual maximum, which won't be a problem given the orthodontic work last year.
Eye exam reminder for September with the local optometrist

Iron levels came back low (65, should be 80-plus) at the last bloodwork — doctor wants to recheck in 3 months with a dietary intervention rather than starting supplements right away. Plan: increase iron-rich foods (red meat 1-2x per week, lentils plus spinach plus tofu rotation, fortified cereal at breakfast occasionally), drink vitamin-C-containing juice with iron-rich meals to boost absorption, avoid coffee within an hour of iron-heavy meals.

Yoga twice a week is the new exercise target for spring. Schedule: Tuesday evening hatha class at the studio near work (downward-time friendly), Saturday morning vinyasa at the bigger studio across town. Goal is consistency through end of June, then evaluate whether to keep this cadence or shift to once-weekly yoga plus a second weekly strength session. The running stays 2-3x per week separately; yoga is additive, not replacement.
Sleep target: 7 hours minimum on weeknights
Mom's birthday is March 12
Mom's birthday: March 12 every year
Mom turns another year older on March 12
Our wedding anniversary is October 20
Wedding anniversary: October 20 each year

Dad's retirement party scheduled for August 16 at the lake house. Guest list is around 40 people including extended family and close work friends from his 35 years at the company. We are doing a casual afternoon-to-evening format with grilling, lawn games for the cousins, and a short toast at sunset where his work team will say something. Mom is handling catering coordination, my sister is handling decorations, and I am on logistics for travel for the out-of-town family.
Niece graduates high school in June this year

Cousin's wedding in Boston the second weekend of September. Ceremony Saturday afternoon at the church on Beacon Hill, reception at the harbor venue Saturday evening. We are flying in Friday morning, staying at the hotel block they reserved on Charles Street through Sunday afternoon. Dress code is cocktail attire. The whole family is going except Uncle Greg who is dealing with knee surgery recovery. Wedding registry is at the usual two stores.
Family reunion every two years — next one is 2027 in Colorado

Sister's new address: 412 Maple Ave, Madison WI, effective the second weekend of June. She and her partner are moving from the apartment downtown to a small house in the Westmorland neighborhood — closer to her work and with a yard for the dog. Move-in is over 3 days; she is renting a truck and asked if I can fly out to help with the heavy stuff that Saturday. Booked the flight last week.
Grandma's care facility visiting hours: 10am to 7pm daily

Recap of the family reunion weekend in Colorado from June 2026, written up the Monday after as a memory dump for future reference. Everyone arrived Friday by 6pm except Aunt Linda whose flight from Boston was delayed three hours; Uncle Greg drove out to Denver International to pick her up around 9:30pm. Saturday morning we had the big breakfast at the lodge — eggs, bacon, the cinnamon-roll thing Grandma always makes but this time my cousin Sarah made it because Grandma's knees were giving her trouble and she did not want to stand at the counter for an hour. Spent Saturday afternoon at the hiking trail near the cabin — about 4 miles loop, easy gradient except the last half-mile which had some scramble; the kids loved it, two of the younger ones (Emma age 7 and Jake age 5) needed help on the rocky section but otherwise fine. Saturday evening was the big group dinner at the steakhouse in town — 22 of us, they put two long tables together, dinner ran until almost 11pm because everyone was telling stories. Uncle Tom told the one about the boat trip in 1987 that nobody had heard in years; Grandma corrected three details and we all laughed. Sunday morning was the memorial for Grandpa — fifth anniversary this year. We did it at the spot by the river where we scattered his ashes; everyone took turns sharing one memory, and Grandma read the Mary Oliver poem that was at the original service. Cousin Mike's kids met some of the family for the first time which was great. Sunday afternoon the older kids went tubing on the river, the adults sat on the deck and Aunt Linda made the green-chile thing that her mother used to make. Drove home Monday morning, hit some weather in Kansas but otherwise uneventful. Action items from the weekend that came up in conversation: (1) Grandma needs help getting the back-stair railing fixed — Mike said he could do it next time he is in town in August, (2) cousin Sarah is moving to Portland in October and wants visitors, (3) the next reunion is 2028 and Aunt Linda volunteered to host in Boston which surprised everyone because she has not hosted since 2010. Photos are on the family Google Drive; I tagged everyone I could recognize.
Rent is due on the 1st of every month
Pay rent by the 1st of each month
Rent due date is always the 1st of the month
Netflix subscription charges 15.99 dollars monthly
Netflix monthly fee: 15.99 dollars on the credit card

Annual budget review yesterday — captured the household monthly subscriptions and bills for the new year's planning. Comcast for internet plus cable is the biggest fixed bill at 89 dollars per month, locked in at that rate after the spring renegotiation when I threatened to switch to the new fiber competitor and they offered the loyalty discount. The package is the 800 Mbps internet tier plus the basic cable channels we barely watch — we keep cable mostly for the sports my partner watches but should probably re-evaluate next year. Electric bill averages 145 dollars per month over the year with seasonal swings (lower in spring and fall, higher in summer with AC). Streaming services tracked separately: Netflix 15.99 dollars, Hulu 11.99 dollars, Disney-plus 10.99 dollars, HBO Max 14.99 dollars — total streaming is roughly Comcast cable cost so the value comparison is direct. Cell phone is 75 dollars per month on the family plan with my partner. Insurance bills (auto, home, umbrella) paid annually so they do not show in the monthly grid. Total monthly fixed: about 1,150 dollars not counting rent plus groceries plus variable spending. Goal for the year is to drop streaming subscriptions we do not actually watch — the audit showed we used Hulu 3 times in the last 6 months.

Reviewed the monthly statement breakdown for the household after the bill came in higher than expected this cycle. Comcast for internet plus cable came in at 109 dollars per month, up from where it had been — the loyalty discount that was supposed to last 12 months expired without me noticing because I missed the renewal email in the spam folder. Need to call retention next week and either restart the loyalty negotiation or seriously evaluate switching to the fiber competitor that has been advertising 1 Gbps for 70 dollars per month with no contract. The 800 Mbps tier on Comcast is more bandwidth than we actually use day-to-day so a step-down would also be an option. Other subscription review notes from the audit: streaming services totaling 54 dollars per month is hard to justify against actual viewing patterns — Hulu was used 3 times in 6 months, that one is getting cancelled. Going to also drop HBO Max after the current month since we have finished the show we signed up for. Net plan: cancel two streaming services, renegotiate Comcast back down, target 150 dollars per month reduction in fixed subscription spending. Will revisit in 90 days to confirm the changes stuck.
Roth IRA contribution limit for 2026 is 7,000 dollars

Annual HOA fee is 850 dollars, due in February. Covers shared landscaping, the pool maintenance, snow removal on the common areas, and the contribution to the reserve fund. Board meeting in January will discuss the proposed dues increase for next year — the reserve fund is below the recommended balance per the most recent reserve study, so dues might bump 5-7 percent next cycle. Set the calendar reminder for the Feb 15 payment deadline.

Auto insurance renewal is in May — should shop around this cycle rather than auto-renew like last time. Current carrier raised the rate 9 percent last year without any claims, which is more than the regional average suggested. Plan: get quotes from three competitors (the usual three with the best regional reviews), keep the same coverage levels and deductibles for an apples-to-apples comparison. Worth bundling the home and auto for a discount if any of them offer a better combined rate.
Tax filing extension granted to October 15 this year

Emergency fund target: 6 months of household expenses, which works out to roughly 42k dollars at current spending levels. Current balance is around 28k dollars, so we are about two-thirds there. Plan to direct half of the next two bonus cycles into the high-yield savings account at the better rate to get us to target. Worth re-evaluating the target every 12 months — household spending creeps and 6-month coverage today might be 5-month coverage in two years if we are not careful.
Want to learn Spanish this year

Spanish learning is on the plan for this year — committing to it properly rather than the dabble-occasionally pattern. Approach: Duolingo daily for streak-keeping habit (15-20 min during morning commute), Pimsleur audio lessons during weekend errands for listening plus speaking practice (one 30-min lesson per week), and one weekly conversation session via italki with a tutor based in Madrid. Target: comfortable for travel-level conversation by November when we are going to Spain. Will re-evaluate at the 3-month mark whether the cadence is sticking.

Reflection on the Spanish learning project at the 4-month mark, written for my own reference and to course-correct if needed. The Duolingo daily streak has held — 122 days — which I am proud of, but I am increasingly aware that Duolingo's design optimizes for engagement and habit-formation rather than fluency progression. My vocabulary is broader than it was but my actual ability to construct sentences in conversation is still limited because I am matching prompts rather than generating from intent. The Pimsleur audio has been more useful — the spaced-repetition plus speak-aloud format builds the kind of pattern-matching reflexes that actually translate to spoken Spanish. The italki tutor sessions are the highest-leverage hour of the week by a meaningful margin; the structured conversation practice with immediate feedback is what is moving me forward. Going to rebalance the time allocation: drop Duolingo to 5 min per day just to keep the streak (or honestly maybe drop it entirely), double the italki sessions to twice a week, add 20-30 min of Spanish-language podcasts during exercise time. Target is still Spain travel in November and I want to feel genuinely capable in restaurants, taxis, hotel check-ins, and basic small-talk. The longer-term ambition is to read a novel in Spanish by next summer.
Running a 5k every weekend as the new exercise habit
Weekly 5k run on Saturdays as the exercise routine

Reading goal: 24 books this year, 2 per month average. Tracking on Goodreads for the streak motivation. Current pace: 9 books finished through April, slightly behind the 8 per month rolling pace, will need to push a bit in May plus June with shorter books. Mix this year is roughly half fiction half non-fiction, with the non-fiction skewing toward science writing and biography. Library hold queue keeps the cost down.
Finished knitting class at the community center last week

Want to try indoor rock climbing this fall — there is a new gym that opened on the east side which has a beginner-friendly bouldering wall plus top-rope routes for when I move up. Plan: take the intro class to get the basic technique and safety briefing, then a 3-month membership to see if it sticks before committing to anything longer. Friend J climbs there regularly and offered to be a starting belay partner.
Garden tomatoes — try heirloom varieties next spring

Photography hobby getting more attention this year — picked up a 50mm prime lens for portrait work which has been a big upgrade from the kit zoom. The f/1.4 aperture lets me work with the available light at the local botanical garden without bumping ISO past 800 which keeps the noise down. Also debated the macro 100mm but held off for now; will revisit after I have used the 50mm enough to know whether the macro use case is real.
Pottery wheel class starts in March at the local studio

Practicing guitar 20 minutes a day on weeknights — small daily discipline rather than the once-a-week-for-an-hour pattern that does not actually build skill. Working through the fingerstyle arrangement of one specific song I want to learn for a family gathering in November. Focus this month is alternating-thumb pattern at slow tempo, will speed up once the fingers know the moves. Practice happens after dinner in the living room with the kids occasionally singing along.

Photography session journal from the Saturday at the botanical garden, captured for future reference on technique and equipment learnings. Brought the 50mm prime plus the 24-70 zoom plus the macro 100mm. Most useful was the macro 100mm — the conservatory had the orchid display set up and the texture detail was incredible at f/2.8, though I had to focus-stack three shots for the deeper flowers because the depth-of-field was too thin. The 50mm was the right choice for the outdoor garden paths because the late-afternoon light through the tree canopy needed the f/1.4 aperture to handle without bumping ISO past 800. Zoom stayed in the bag most of the day — useful only for the two architectural shots of the greenhouse glass roof, but I could have done those with the 50mm and walked back further. Settings that worked: aperture priority mode most of the day, ISO auto with max 1600, exposure compensation -0.3 for the high-contrast scenes with bright sky behind dark leaves. Settings that did not work: tried manual mode for the macro work, ended up with three overexposed shots before switching to aperture priority and adjusting EC for the rest. Color profile: shot in RAW plus JPEG, used the standard JPEG profile because the LCD review is more reliable that way, will re-edit the RAWs at home in Lightroom. Tripod was overkill — went only when shooting the macro stuff. Next time bring fewer lenses (the 50mm plus macro 100mm only) and skip the zoom entirely; the weight saving is meaningful at 4 hours of walking. Composition learnings: the rule of thirds works well for the wider garden shots but the macro work is better with center composition because the subject is what you want the viewer to look at, and rule-of-thirds creates negative space that distracts when the subject is already small. Sharpness check: the 50mm at f/2 is noticeably softer than at f/4 — I had been shooting wide open out of habit but the f/2 results are losing detail in the petals. Will shoot the next macro session at f/4 minimum and re-evaluate. Plan for next session: orchid show at the conservatory next month, target 30-40 keeper shots, will set a focus-stacking workflow in advance so I am not improvising on the fly.
